fields.name: INS Vikramaditya
fields.history:
INS Vikramaditya's story actually begins in the Soviet era. It was originally built as Admiral Gorshkov in the 1980s for the Soviet Navy. After the collapse of the Soviet Union in 1991, Russia found it expensive to maintain large warships, and the vessel was eventually decommissioned.

In the early 2000s, India—looking to strengthen its naval aviation—signed a deal with Russia to acquire and rebuild the ship. The agreement (2004) included extensive refurbishment, converting it from a hybrid cruiser-carrier into a full STOBAR aircraft carrier (Short Take-Off But Arrested Recovery).

However, the refit became one of the most complex naval upgrade projects in history:

Costs increased significantly beyond initial estimates
Delivery was delayed multiple times due to technical challenges
Major systems like boilers and flight deck required redesign

After years of trials and modifications, the ship was finally commissioned into the Indian Navy in 2013.

Strategic Importance (Expanded)
Helped India maintain continuous aircraft carrier capability
Strengthened India's presence in the Indian Ocean Region
Played a key role in training naval aviators for carrier operations
Acts as the core of a Carrier Battle Group (CBG)
Key part of India's carrier battle group

fields.name: INS Vikrant
fields.history:
Detailed History

INS Vikrant (IAC-1) represents one of India's most ambitious defence projects. The idea of building an indigenous aircraft carrier was proposed in the late 1990s, but actual construction began in 2009 at
Cochin Shipyard Limited in Kochi.

The project faced multiple challenges:

Delays in procurement of steel and technology
Design complexities (India had no prior experience building carriers)
Budget and timeline extensions

Despite this, India developed significant domestic capability:

Indigenous warship-grade steel (by Steel Authority of India Limited)
Advanced combat systems integration
Indigenous aviation facilities

Sea trials began in 2021, and the ship was officially commissioned in 2022 by Narendra Modi.

Strategic Importance 
Marks India's entry into an elite group of nations that can design and build aircraft carriers
Boosts Atmanirbhar Bharat (self-reliance in defence)
Provides long-term independence from foreign suppliers
Strengthens maritime dominance in the Indo-Pacific

fields.name: INS Arihant
fields.history:
INS Arihant is the result of decades of secret development under India's Advanced Technology Vessel (ATV) Project, which began in the 1970s. The project received major momentum in the 1990s with technical assistance from Russia.

Key milestones:

Launched in 2009 in Visakhapatnam
Underwent years of nuclear reactor testing and sea trials
Officially commissioned in 2016

The submarine uses a nuclear reactor, allowing it to stay submerged for months without surfacing.

Most importantly, Arihant completed India's nuclear triad, which includes:

Land-based missiles
Air-delivered weapons
Sea-based nuclear weapons
Strategic Importance 
Provides credible second-strike capability (even after a nuclear attack)
Ensures deterrence stability
Operates in stealth, making detection extremely difficult
One of India's most classified and secure defence assets

fields.name: INS Kolkata
fields.history:
INS Kolkata is the lead ship of the Kolkata-class destroyers (Project 15A). Built at
Mazagon Dock Shipbuilders Limited in Mumbai, it reflects India's growing capability in building large warships.

Construction began in the early 2000s, but the ship faced:

Delays due to technology integration
Cost overruns
Complexity in modern weapon systems

It was finally commissioned in 2014.

The ship incorporates:

Stealth features to reduce radar detection
Advanced radar systems (like MF-STAR)
Long-range missiles including BrahMos
 Strategic Importance 
Acts as a multi-role combat platform
Provides air defence, anti-ship, and anti-submarine warfare
Protects carrier groups and naval assets
One of the most heavily armed destroyers in the Indian Navy

fields.name: INS Visakhapatnam
fields.history:
Detailed History

INS Visakhapatnam is the lead ship of the advanced Project 15B destroyers, an upgraded version of the Kolkata class.

Built again at Mazagon Dock Shipbuilders Limited, the ship incorporates major improvements:

Better stealth design
Advanced electronic warfare systems
Improved automation

The ship was commissioned in 2021 and represents the next generation of Indian destroyers.

 Strategic Importance 
Equipped with BrahMos missile, one of the fastest cruise missiles in the world
Enhanced survivability in modern warfare
Plays a key role in network-centric warfare
Strengthens India's blue-water navy capability

fields.name: INS Tabar
fields.history:
Detailed History

INS Tabar is part of the Talwar-class frigates built in Russia and inducted into the Indian Navy in 2004.

The ship gained international attention during anti-piracy operations in the Gulf of Aden (2008). It successfully:

Engaged and destroyed a pirate "mother ship"
Protected Indian and international merchant vessels

Since then, it has been actively deployed in:

Anti-piracy patrols
Joint naval exercises
Escort missions
Strategic Importance 
Proven combat experience in real-world missions
Versatile in anti-air, anti-surface, and anti-submarine roles
Important for safeguarding international trade routes
